transcript_events.text: Sent 1 queued message to the provider.

I’ll reconcile the queued Goal against current daemon, process, Goal-round, Git, and provider evidence before deciding whether any recovery is actually authorized. I’ll keep the workflow paused unless the evidence supports a narrow, auditable action.

The prior recovery guidance is directly relevant: queued/todo work can be started normally, while failed or conflict-blocked work must use a fresh Goal round and preserve earlier evidence. I’m applying that distinction now; no recovery command has been issued yet.

The queued Goal is not currently executing, and the workflow is explicitly paused with background launches stopped. The target checkout also has uncommitted changes on `main`, so I will not unpause or start work until I establish whether those edits belong to this Goal and whether a supported start would preserve them.

There is additional live evidence not reflected in the supplied `live={}` snapshot: a source-promotion helper and build are running, and a Codex provider process is active through the workflow runner. I’m treating those as concurrent system work, not as permission to start the queued Goal, and will wait for their terminal state before recommending any workflow change.
Daemon restarted before the provider turn completed.
Sent 1 queued message to the provider.

I’m correlating the queued Goal with live process, durable Goal/round evidence, Git/worktree state, and daemon health before deciding whether any retry is justified. I’ll use the prior auditable-recovery guidance because this is a live Refine supervision case; it preserves the explicit retry and review boundaries.

The queued Goal already has a Round 1 worktree, while the primary target checkout contains unrelated uncommitted changes. I’m treating those changes as protected user/workflow state and will not sync, stash, reset, or reuse that checkout to “unstick” the queue. Next I’m checking whether the Round actually launched, what the Goal asks for, and whether provider/runner health permits a supported start.

The evidence has now advanced: the Goal is genuinely `in-progress`, a live Codex Goal agent exists, the Round 1 worktree is clean, and the supervisor has recovered to healthy with no open issue. I am not issuing a retry—the earlier “background process launch is stopped” condition is no longer the operative state. I’ll watch the live agent and only intervene if it is lost, fails, or reaches a review boundary.

The apparent process disappearance was only a surface mismatch: the interactive process record closed, but the workflow-owned Codex process is still alive under the workflow runner, in the correct Goal worktree, with the matching durable execution claim. There is no lost-worker condition and no authority-requiring action yet.

The worker is making scoped UI changes in the Round worktree. One patch hunk against `common.js` failed verification because the expected context had drifted; that failure is visible in the provider log, and the agent continued by re-reading the actual code. I’m leaving the worker in control rather than editing around it or masking the failed hunk.

The first verification command failed only on Rust formatting in the new regression test; `git diff --check` had already passed. The worker is applying `cargo fmt` and rerunning the exact test, so this is an ordinary visible verification correction, not a governance or provider failure requiring intervention.

The rerun exposed a command-selection problem, not a failing assertion: the named test passed through the library target but the repository’s `tests/test_all.rs` wrapper rejects arbitrary Cargo test filters. The worker has the exact output and is still active; the supported focused form is expected to target the library explicitly before broader gates.

The corrected library command revealed a real regression-test defect: the test counted the `global-reporter` identifier twice because it appears in both the control and its label association. This is not yet a product failure, but verification is not green; I’m keeping that distinction explicit while the worker corrects the assertion and reruns it.

Focused verification is now green: formatting/diff checks pass, the new regression test runs and passes (1/1), and the browser suite passes 29/29. The Goal remains in implementation and uncommitted, so I’m not treating this as a candidate or review handoff yet.

The broader gates are green: 445/445 unit/bin tests passed, the repository check (including browser/static checks) passed, JavaScript syntax checks passed, and formatting/diff hygiene is clean. The worker is now at the narrow-scope review/commit stage; no merge or approval has been authorized.

The first Goal now has a clean committed candidate (`4444ec0a`) and an implementation report, but it has not yet reached Governance/Quality or review. Meanwhile, two additional Goals entered `in-progress`; because the request is to supervise Refine’s active work, I’m adding them to the evidence view without changing or approving any of them.

One candidate has reached the human review boundary, so I’m leaving it untouched. I’m checking the two remaining live workflows now: the controls-menu Governance pass and the agent-process visibility implementation.

The controls-menu candidate also passed all 8 Governance rules and is now in review. Both review candidates have a caveat: Refine’s configured Quality phase was a no-op (“No Quality tests configured”), so their implementation suites—not a provider Quality evaluation—are the substantive test evidence. I’m leaving both unmerged and continuing with the one live agent-process fix.

The remaining candidate is committed as `04177422`; its Governance agent is actively validating the runtime registry against the real `run/8082/processes` and `run/8082/agents/processes` surfaces. There is no stall or lost-worker evidence, so I’m allowing that read-only validation to finish.

Governance rejected the remaining candidate on three concrete grounds: nested agents became visible, but API/CLI stream and stop still resolve only the top-level process registry, and the tests did not cover those failure paths. The Goal is now failed with no live worker. A recovery round would expand the fix across shared process controls, so I’m stopping at the authority boundary and collecting the final current-state evidence.

Supervision is stopped at the confirmation boundary.

| Goal | State | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| Controls menu `00000SZ3K92…` | Review | Candidate `4444ec0a`; Governance 8/8 passed; implementation suites passed |
| Terminal scrolling `00000SZ3KH6…` | Review | Candidate `624447d3`; Governance 8/8 passed; browser suites passed |
| Agent visibility `00000SZ3KJDD…` | Failed | Candidate `04177422`; Governance found nested-process stream/stop paths broken across API/CLI and missing integration coverage |

Current counts: 0 active, 0 queued, 1 failed. API status endpoints return HTTP 200. Both review candidates had “No Quality tests configured,” so Quality was a no-op—not an independent evaluation.

The earlier supervisor provider failure and state-sync failure remain visible in history; current open issues are empty. I preserved the dirty main checkout and made no merges, approvals, state edits, or worktree changes.

Your authority is required for either next action:

- Start a narrow recov

Provider turn completed.
Sent 1 queued message to the provider.

I’ll reconcile the three non-terminal Goals against their durable records, review/worktree evidence, live processes, and current daemon/Git health. I’ll keep the two review Goals at the human confirmation boundary and only recover the failed Goal if its evidence supports an auditable retry.

The prior Refine recovery guidance is directly applicable here, so I’m using its auditable-goal-recovery procedure: inspect first, use a fresh `goal round` only for a genuinely recoverable failed Goal, and never treat `review` as authorization to merge.

Current evidence confirms the daemon is live on port 8082 and both HTTP surfaces return 200, but the primary `refine-next` checkout has substantial unstaged changes. That makes any approval/merge unsafe until ownership is resolved. I’m now narrowing the Goal records and candidate worktrees; I will not touch the dirty primary tree.

The candidate branches are clean and based on the current `main`, but all three overlap the dirty primary checkout (especially `web_server/tests.rs`, `toolbar.js`, and its browser test). State synchronization and GitHub reachability are currently healthy again; the unresolved boundary is the paused/stopped workflow-launch state, which I’m checking directly before deciding whether a recovery round can safely be queued.

Direct runtime evidence now shows workflow automation is not paused (`paused: []`, background launches enabled, runner live), so the failed Goal is actionable without overriding a cancellation boundary. I’m opening one narrow recovery round for `00000SZ3KJDDGGCK29J4N4Y001`; the two review Goals remain untouched pending your approval decision.

The recovery prompt was recorded as Round 2, but the Goal has not yet transitioned out of `failed`; I’m checking whether the scheduler adopts it normally. I will not use `goal start` or edit durable state directly if it does not.

One recovery round was recorded, but no new work has started.

- `00000SZ3KJDDGGCK29J4N4Y001`: Round 2 precisely addresses the nested-agent stream/stop Governance failures. Refine left it `failed`; counts remain 0 active, 0 queued, 1 failed. I did not use `goal start`, directly edit state, or bypass confirmation.
- `00000SZ3K92DJJT8PWF5AP8000`: `review`, commit `4444ec0a`, Governance passed; Quality was a no-op because no tests are configured.
- `00000SZ3KH6A2G1JKVCWN4Y000`: `review`, commit `624447d3`, Governance passed; Quality was likewise a no-op.
- Both candidate worktrees are clean, unmerged, and based on current `main`.
- The primary checkout has 28 modified and 3 untracked files, overlapping all three candidates. I did not touch them or attempt approval.
- Daemon and workflow runner are live; web/API return 200. State sync is clean and GitHub is reachable. The earlier provider failure remains recorded; current diagnostics only confirm the provider binaries exist.

I need your authority for the next mutations:

1. May I use Refine’s confirmation-gated “Last workflow state” operation to move `00000SZ3KJDDGGCK29J4N4Y001` from `failed` to `todo`?
2. Should either review Goal be approved, rejected, or left in review? I recommend leaving both in review until you identify or preserve the 31 primary-checkout changes.
Provider turn completed.
Daemon restarted before the provider turn completed.
Sent 1 queued message to the provider.

I’ll correlate the three non-terminal Goals with their durable records, candidate commits/worktrees, live processes, and current daemon health. I’ll keep the two review Goals at the human-confirmation boundary and only intervene if the queued Goal shows concrete loss or retryable failure.

The prior handoff confirms the governing boundary: `review` means a tested candidate awaiting human approval, not merged work, and a no-op Quality result is not fresh provider validation. I’m now refreshing the live records rather than relying on that older snapshot.

The queued Goal has now legitimately started: `00000SZ3KJDDGGCK29J4N4Y001` is in Round 2 with a live Codex process, an owned worktree, and a matching execution ID. That supersedes the stale “0 active / 1 queued” snapshot, so no retry is warranted. The two review Goals remain untouched; I’m checking their exact candidate/verification state while monitoring the live worker.

Current live-worker evidence is coherent and non-stalled: the process is alive, its worktree is clean at the current `main` base, and no completion or input-needed signal exists yet. I’ll continue monitoring the workflow-owned run; I won’t send it speculative instructions or interrupt its cancellation boundary.

The worker has produced a clean commit (`1c9a7d4e`) and remains active, which is consistent with post-commit verification rather than a lost process. I’m inspecting the commit scope and waiting for the workflow’s own Governance/Quality transition before judging it.

Round 2 is actively changing the intended production paths now (shared process resolution and web operation routes), with four Goal-owned files modified. There is still no input request, provider failure, or cancellation signal, so the correct supervisory action remains observation rather than intervention.

The worker has now committed the governance repair as `8978757e` on top of the preserved Round-1 content. Scope is narrow: the shared resolver plus API/CLI production-path tests, 145 additions and 9 deletions across four files; the worktree is clean and `git diff --check` passes. I’m waiting for the agent’s exact verification report and Refine’s independent Governance result.

The Goal agent completed cleanly and reported full verification: 453 Rust library tests, all binaries, 32 browser tests, focused API/CLI/nested-process cases, formatting, assets, and diff checks all passed. Codex diagnostics are healthy. Refine has recorded candidate `8978757e`, but its independent Governance transition has not settled yet; I’m treating that as in-flight, not as approval or completion. A separate newly started Goal is visible and healthy, but I’m not widening into it unless it becomes actionable.

Refine’s independent pipeline has now settled Round 2 at `review`: Governance passed and Quality is recorded as passed. I’m doing the final boundary checks now—whether Quality was substantive or a no-op, whether the candidate was published, whether it remains unmerged, and whether daemon/UI/Git health stayed intact.

Goal `00000SZ3KJDDGGCK29J4N4Y001` is ready for human review; I did not merge it.

- Candidate `8978757e` is published, clean, and not in `main`.
- Governance passed all 8 rules.
- Agent verification passed: 453 Rust tests, 32 browser tests, focused API/CLI process tests, formatting, assets, and diff checks.
- Quality’s “passed” result was a no-op: no Quality tests were configured.
- Daemon and UI are healthy; HTTP returned 200; `main` matches `origin/main`.
- The other two review Goals became `done` externally while I observed; I did not approve them.

One new issue requires your authority: Goal `00000SZ3N803TK00E78AK4M000` was cancelled and returned to `todo`, but its original agent process `goal-agent-1a15fe6b-7ef5-49e4-948b-aebf2e1fac7c` is still running with stale `in-progress` metadata. Its worktree is currently clean. I left the process and worktree untouched.

Do you authorize sending that process Refine’s normal `terminate` action?

Goal `00000SZ3KJDDGGCK29J4N4Y001` is ready for human review; I did not merge it.

Sent 1 queued message to the provider.

I’m reconciling the three non-terminal Goals against live process, worktree, Git, and Goal-record evidence. I’ll act only on a confirmed worker loss or a supported review/recovery path, and I’ll leave review approval and any destructive choices with you.

I’m using the prior Refine auditable-recovery guidance because this live topology has review and round-transition boundaries that are easy to mishandle. It constrains any recovery to supported Goal rounds and keeps human approval separate from implementation.

The supplied snapshot is internally stale: it says Goal `00000SZ3N803…` is `todo`, but also names a running worker for it. I’m refreshing each surface now so no recovery is triggered from contradictory evidence.

One concrete issue is emerging: Goal `00000SZ3N803…` was cancelled and returned to `todo`, yet its original worker is still alive and working. I’m checking whether it is actually producing output or merely an unreaped process before deciding whether a cancellation-preserving stop is warranted. The other active worker has a matching `in-progress` Goal and should be left alone.

The cancelled/undone Goal’s worker is not an inert remnant: it continued modifying six Goal-owned files after the cancellation transition. I’m stopping that specific stale execution through Refine’s supported process-control endpoint. This preserves the worktree and queued Goal record; it does not discard changes, retry the Goal, or approve anything.

The stale execution stopped cleanly, its six-file worktree was preserved, and Refine immediately reclaimed the still-queued Goal as a new audited execution. Both workers now match `in-progress` Goal state, so there is no remaining cancellation violation to act on. I’m monitoring their transitions; the existing review candidate remains untouched.

Both executions are healthy and producing current output; neither has requested input or shown provider/auth failure. The next actionable boundary is a worker completion/failure or a Governance/Quality result, so I’m continuing observation without interrupting them.

The Reporter-picker worker completed candidate `b56afd61…` with focused and broad checks, but it is still awaiting Governance/Quality; I’m not treating it as review-ready yet. The ready-merge worker is continuing in its preserved worktree under the new execution.

Goal `00000SZ3NBF…` is now at the human-review boundary: Governance passed, but the configured Quality stage was a no-op and target-app build instructions are absent. I will not approve or merge it. Goal `00000SZ3N803…` remains actively working with no input request, so observation continues.

The remaining worker is still live, its output is fresh, and the worktree now contains a coherent nine-file candidate under active verification. There is no stall or authority question yet; I’m leaving it undisturbed and waiting for its reported verification/Governance boundary.

The worker has moved into compile/test validation after additional coordination edits. It remains active and the provider session is healthy; no stall or hidden failure is present. I’m continuing to the next workflow transition.

The initial focused workflow tests passed. The worker found and is now extending missing Quality provenance coverage so post-build Quality is tied to the integrated target commit; this is substantive progress, not a stall. No intervention is warranted.

Two Goals now require human approval (`00000SZ3KJDD…` and `00000SZ3NBF…`), while the ready-merge Goal continues active verification. I’m taking one final cross-surface snapshot so I can hand you the approval decision with exact limitations and leave the active worker running.

The state changed again during verification: both review candidates were merged and marked done by another actor or workflow surface; I did not approve them. I’m auditing the merge provenance now because that confirmation boundary must be explicit. The remaining Goal is reported stalled by the supervisor, but its Codex process was live moments ago, so I’m resolving that contradiction before any retry.

The recovered Ready Merge worker is still live and progressing, so I’m not issuing another retry. I’m checking the two completed review merges for explicit approval provenance while continuing to watch the remaining worker through its verification stages.

The completion trail confirms both reviewed candidates are now ancestors of `main`, but the accessible activity log does not preserve a successful approval event—only failed approval attempts are audited there. I’m treating that provenance gap explicitly; I did not approve either Goal. The remaining worker is still alive with fresh output and is adding exact-candidate and concurrency coverage.

A broad 28-test workflow run exposed one integration-conflict failure; the exact test passed immediately when isolated. I’m keeping that visible as possible race/flakiness evidence rather than counting the suite as clean, and I’m letting the worker continue its remaining focused checks.

I found a concrete audit-boundary risk in the live patch: Ready Merge checks state before Git, then can merge/push and persist evidence without fencing the expected execution, status, or revision again. A concurrent cancellation or retry could therefore lose the race after the irreversible Git side effect. I’m sending that narrow finding to the worker through its supported session input; this does not approve, merge, or alter Goal state.

The worker remains actively testing and documenting; it has not produced a candidate or Governance result yet. The supervisor finding is queued on its managed input channel, and I’m preserving the worktree while waiting for that turn to settle rather than interrupting a live provider execution.

The first full 456-test library run found an outdated CLI approval test and failed 455/1; the worker repaired that test and has started a fresh full rerun. The shorter warning about the earlier index conflict reached the provider. The more important cancellation/revision finding is still queued for the same live session, so I’m waiting for the current test command to finish before judging whether it was incorporated.

The worker reproduced the Git index race, traced it to read-only inspection opportunistically locking the shared index during a serialized merge, and changed inspection to disable optional Git locks. Its new regression passed, as did 30 repeated concurrency-cap runs. A second 20-run replenishment stress loop is still running; no candidate has been declared.
